coredata.dc:title: Facile and green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from the flowers of Abelmoschus manihot (Linn.) Medicus for sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wan, Yuyan
Wang, Min
Zhang, Kailian
Fu, Qifeng
Gao, Manjie
Wang, Lujun
Xia, Zhining
Gao, Die
coredata.dc:description: A new type of carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method by using the flowers of Abelmoschus manihot (Linn.) Medicus as precursors for the first time. The CDs exhibited strong blue emission with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 30.8%. Further, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was found to be rapidly and sensitively quenched by 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), an environmental pollutant. The linear response of TNP ranged from 25 nM to 40 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5 nM. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching effect of TNP on CDs was much higher than those for other competitive compounds and metal ions. The results demonstrated that CDs had high selectivity to TNP. Furthermore, the CDs were successfully applied for the determination of TNP in tap water and river water with the recoveries ranging from 92.6% to 113% (RSD < 5%). These results demonstrated that the CDs can be applied to the selective and sensitive detection of TNP in real samples. Finally, the CDs were explored for cytotoxicity in three cell lines and cellular imaging in Hela cells. Benefited from the good biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity, the CDs had great potential for the application in biological field.

head.ce:title: Facile and green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from the flowers of Abelmoschus manihot (Linn.) Medicus for sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol and cellular imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new type of carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method by using the flowers of Abelmoschus manihot (Linn.) Medicus as precursors for the first time. The CDs exhibited strong blue emission with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 30.8%. Further, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was found to be rapidly and sensitively quenched by 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), an environmental pollutant. The linear response of TNP ranged from 25 nM to 40 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5 nM. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching effect of TNP on CDs was much higher than those for other competitive compounds and metal ions. The results demonstrated that CDs had high selectivity to TNP. Furthermore, the CDs were successfully applied for the determination of TNP in tap water and river water with the recoveries ranging from 92.6% to 113% (RSD < 5%). These results demonstrated that the CDs can be applied to the selective and sensitive detection of TNP in real samples. Finally, the CDs were explored for cytotoxicity in three cell lines and cellular imaging in Hela cells. Benefited from the good biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity, the CDs had great potential for the application in biological field.

ce:keyword.ce:text: The flowers of Abelmoschus manihot (Linn.) Medicus

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of industry, the problems of environmental pollution such as heavy metals pollution [1], bisphenol [2,3] and nitro-aromatic compounds pollution in different environmental samples, become more and more serious. Nitro-aromatic compounds, especially nitrophenols, have been found to have numerous applications in chemical industry and military industry [4,5]. They are generally used in the preparation of pharmaceuticals, dyes, various pesticides and explosives [6–8]. However, these compounds are also well known deleterious compounds. Due to their overuse, free dumping and degradation is difficult in environment, they can cause serious pollution to environment especially to lakes, rivers, ground water and soils [9–11]. Moreover, it is reported that exposing to even trace concentrations of these compounds may lead to serious health problem such as eye damage, respiratory systems and metabolic disorders [12,13]. 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP, also named picric acid) which is used as a powerful explosive in landmines, a chemical intermediate in dye manufacturing industry and a fungicide in agricultural and medical fields, is one of the representative nitrophenols [14]. In recent years, with the constant development of urbanization and industrialization, the chance of producing TNP in industrial wastewater, soils etc. gradually increased [15]. Moreover, as the result of high water solubility, poor biodegradability, toxicity and highly acidity due to nitro groups with strong electron-withdrawing groups, TNP has already caused certain pollution to the environment [16,17]. The more serious problem is that trace TNP can cause severe skin irritation and other tremendous health problems such as dizziness, nausea or other symptoms consequently [12,18]. In such circumstances, it is of great significance to develop a simple and effective method to the analysis of trace TNP.

To date, a lot of work to TNP detection has been performed by using different methods including liquid chromatography [19], mass spectrometry [20], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [21], electrochemical method [22], fluorescent methods and etc. [23]. Among these methods, fluorescent method has proven to be one of the most outstanding approaches owing to its advantages in sensitivity, simple sample preparation process, short response time and portability [24]. In recent years, various fluorescent materials such as quantum dots (QDs) [25], polymers [26], nanocluster [27], metal/covalentorganic frameworks [28] and carbon dots (CDs) [29,30] have already been emerged as fluorescent sensor elements for TNP detection. Specially, CDs acting as a new fluorescence sensor element used for detection of TNP have gained much attention.

CDs are a new type of carbon nanomaterials and have been widely used as fluorescent sensor elements in fluorescent detection [31,32]. Due to the outstanding advantages such as simple preparation process, attractive fluorescence properties, good water-solubility, low cost, low toxicity and tunable surface functional groups, CDs are regarded as a rising star of the fluorescent materials family [33,34]. Nowadays, many different CDs which prepared by using different precursors have already been developed for the fluorescence detection of TNP. Although these CDs can be directly used to detect TNP, they suffer from some disadvantages. Firstly, the precursors used are usually chemical reagents such as mandelic acid [35], ethylenediamine [35], malonic acid [36]. The limit of detection (LOD) of these CDs mostly ranged from tens of μM to dozens of nM, and their sensitivity is not particularly satisfactory. Secondly, some even uses toxic solvents such as chloroform [29] and DMF to prepare CDs [18]. The usage of these toxic solvents are not only harmful to the environment but also harmful to human body. Thus, taking water as solvent and natural materials as precursors exhibit amazing advances to the synthesis of CDs, and these approaches also meet the requirements of green chemistry.

To date, various natural materials such as sterculia lychnophora seeds [37,38], Eleusine coracana [39], Tamarindus indica leaves [40] have been applied as the precursors for the preparation of CDs. These green CDs show good optical properties and detection performance. For TNP detection, two different CDs have already been prepared by using natural materials as precursors and water as solvent. Deng's group [41] have successfully prepared CDs based probe for the detection of TNP by using Sichuan Bergamot, a traditional Chinese medicine as precursors. The probe exhibited good sensitivity and selectivity toward TNP from 0.4 μM to 80 μM with a LOD of 82 nM. However, the sensitivity of CDs was not so satisfactory. Li’s group [30] have successfully synthesized CDs with a fluorescence QY of 18.67% by using grape skin as precursors. And TNP quenched the fluorescence of CDs linearly in range of 0.06–79.4 μM with a LOD of 10 nM. The low LOD of CDs demonstrates that CDs have good sensitivity for TNP detection. However, sensitivity still need to be further improved to the trace TNP detection in complex samples. In view that CDs which prepared by using natural materials as green precursors, are rarely used to the detection of TNP, more natural materials need to be developed for the preparation of CDs for the detection of TNP.

As we all know, the tunable surface functional groups which come mainly from the precursors are related to the sensitivity and selectivity of CDs on target molecules [42]. Moreover, for the detection of nitro-aromatic compounds, the principle of choosing materials as precursors is generally based on the existence of compounds with electron-donating groups in materials. And these compounds usually have fluorescence properties [43]. CDs which prepared by using these kinds of materials have electron-donating groups on surface and can be used for the detection of electron-deficiency molecules such as TNP through donor-acceptor (D-A) interactions [44]. Hence, natural materials which are rich in these compounds (with electron-donating groups) can be selected as precursors for the preparation of CDs. It is gratifying that natural materials which mainly contain flavonoids and their glycosides usually can supply many electron-donating groups in CDs [42]. Therefore, it is a good choice to use these natural materials which are rich in flavonoids as green precursors to prepare CDs for further detection of TNP.

The flowers of Abelmoschus manihot (Linn.) Medicus (family Malvaceae), recorded as “Huang Sukui” in China, have many pharmacological activities such as anti-inflammatory, anti-coagulant and diuresis effects [45]. Moreover, flavonoids and flavonoids glycosides (e.g. myricetin, quercetin and their glycosides) are identified as the main constituents in the flowers. And the total flavonoid content reaches up to 37 mg·g−1 [46,47]. The abundance of flavonoids and their glycosides make the flowers of A. manihot. potential natural green precursors for the preparation of CDs. And the CDs may be rich in electron-donating groups and can be applied to the detection of TNP.

In present study, a facile and green hydrothermal method was applied to the preparation of CDs by using the flowers of A.manihot as green precursors for the first time. As CDs which have high fluorescence QY may show high sensitivity for the detection of target compounds [48], the preparation conditions of CDs were firstly optimized to obtain CDs with high QY. The dosage of flowers, reaction temperature and reaction time were optimized and the CDs prepared under optimized conditions exhibited high fluorescence QY up to 30.8%. Further, the CDs were used to detect TNP and the proposed fluorescent method permitted a selective and sensitive detection of TNP in a linear range of 25 nM-40 μM with a LOD of 5 nM. And the fluorescence quenching mechanism between CDs and TNP was identified as FRET and PET. Furthermore, the CDs were applied to detect TNP in real water samples with a recovery range of 92.6–113%, and the results confirmed a good analytical performance of this sensing method. In addition, due to the low toxicity and biocompatibility, the CDs can be also used for cell imaging and can be acted as a fluorescent probe for fluorescence detection of TNP in bio-system.

The dried flowers of A.manihot were purchased from Lotus pond traditional Chinese medicine market (Chengdu, Sichuan). The voucher specimen of the flowers of A.manihot (No.AM20180402) was deposited at the School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University (Sichuan, China). 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP, purity>98%), 2-nitrophenol (2-NP, purity>98%), 3-nitrophenol (3-NP, purity>98%), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP, purity>99%), phenol (purity>98%), 2,4-dinitroaniline (2,4-DNA，purity>98%), p-dihydroxybenzene (p-DHB, purity>98%), o-dihydroxybenzene (o-DHB, purity>98%), 1,2,3-Trihydroxybenzeb (1,2,3-THB, purity>98%) and m-dihydroxybenzene (m-DHB, purity>99%) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP, purity>98%) and 2,4-dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT, purity>98%) were obtained from JACS Chem Product Co., Ltd. (Zhengzhou, China). Quinine sulfate (purity>99%) was bought from Acros Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Human lung cancer (A549), HepG2 and HeLa cell lines were obtained from the Cell Center of the Chinese Academy of Medicinal Sciences (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were obtained from Beijing Dingguo biotechnology co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Gibco (Gaithersburg, MD, USA). All chemicals were of at least analytical grade and all aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water in the whole experiment process.

The UV absorption spectra of CDs were obtained on a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected between 4000 and 500 cm−1 on an IR Affinity-1S FT-IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). The fluorescence spectra of CDs were collected on a LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer). The fluorescence lifetimes were performed on a FLS980 lifetime and steady state spectrometer (Edinburgh Company). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was taken using a JEM 2100 (JEOL Ltd) transmission electron microscope working at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was conducted on an Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (Pabakytucal X′ pert powder), X-ray photoelectronspectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed with an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Electron). MTT was conducted on the synergy H1 microplate reader (Biotek) and fluorescence images were obtained by using IX-73 inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan).

CDs were synthesized by a simple, one-step hydrothermal method using the flowers of A.manihot as green precursors. Firstly, 5 g of the flowers of A.manihot were cut into pieces that can pass through a 10 mesh sieve. Then 75 mL deionized water was added into pieces and ultrasound for 20 min. After that, the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at the temperature of 220 °C for 4 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 20 min and filtered through 0.22 μm membrane filter. The final solution was stored at 4 °C for further experiments.
The QY of CDs was measured by comparing the fluorescence intensities and absorption values of quinine sulfate. Details can be seen in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the stability of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the stability of CDs, the influences of pH, ionic strength and solvent on the stability of CDs were evaluated individually. The evaluation of pH was measured at room temperature in the buffer solution of the CDs (pH 3–10). The influence of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity was evaluated in the presence of NaCl (concentration ranged from 0 to 1.0 M). Different solvents (water, methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile and acetone) were used to disperse CDs and the influences of different solvents on the fluorescence intensities of CDs were evaluated. Moreover, the anti-photobleaching ability of CDs was investigated through continuous irradiation for 10 h. All fluorescence measurements were performed with the excitation and emission wavelength of 330 nm and 410 nm, respectively.

CDs were dispersed in PBS solution (pH = 6) with a concentration of 100 μg·mL−1. Before and after adsorbing TNP, the fluorescence spectra of CDs solution were measured by fluorescence spectroscopy. In order to measure the quenching capacity and competitive recognition ability of CDs on TNP, the obtained dried CDs were dispersed in buffer solution (pH = 6) to get the working solution (100 μg·mL−1). After that, 2.7 mL working solution was added to the 4 mL fluorescent colorimetric tube, and then 300 μL of a certain concentration of TNP was added into the above solution. The fluorescence spectrum of TNP was measured immediately after adequately mixing at room temperature. To evaluate the selectivity of TNP, Pb2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, 2-NP, 3-NP, 4-NP, phenol, 2,4-DNA, 2,4-DNP, 2,4-DNT, p-DHB, o-DHB, m-DHB, 1,2,3-THB and TNP (Structures of compounds were shown in Fig. S1) were added separately to above CDs solutions. The fluorescence intensity of the solution was measured at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm and the emission recorded over the wavelength range of 350–600 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TNP in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Water samples came from the local Zhongshan park lake (Luzhou, Sichuan Province, China) and our laboratory (tap water), respectively. The lake water sample was centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 20 min and then filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filter to remove the insoluble impurities. The tap water sample was heated to eliminate the residual chlorine and then filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filter. A standard addition method was used to evaluate the practical applicability of the sensing platform. Moreover, standard solution with different concentration of TNP was added into the water samples to calculate the recovery of TNP based on the proposed method. The fluorescence intensity was measured by fluorescence spectrometer at the excitation and emission wavelengths of 330 nm and 410 nm, respectively. Under the same experimental conditions, the experiment was repeated for three times to obtain the relative standard deviation (RSD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: MTT assays were used to assess the cytotoxicity of CDs in HepG2, A549, HeLa cells. Firstly, cells were seeded into 96 well plates and incubated for 24 h until confluency. Then, cells were treated with CDs at various concentrations. Next, 10 μL of MTT (5 mg·mL−1) was added to each well and the plate was incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. The treatment medium was removed and DMSO (150 μL) was added to dissolve the formazan complex. Absorbance was detected at 570 nm by using a synergy H1 microplate reader.

For cellular imaging, the HeLa cells were seeded into 6 well plates and incubated for 24 h until confluency. Then, cells were treated with 10 μL of 200 μg·mL−1 of CDs at 37 °C for 3 h.Then, the DMEM medium was removed and the cells were washed by PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) three times. Subsequently, the cells were kept in PBS buffer for fluorescence imaging. Finally, cells loaded CDs were treated with TNP (concentration was 10 μM) in the culture medium for another 1 h at 37 °C for further fluorescence imaging under inverted fluorescence microscopy.
The applications of CDs were shown in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and optical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, CDs were prepared through one-pot hydrothermal treatment process by using flowers of A.manihot as natural green precursors at the temperature of 220 °C for 4 h. The products were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to obtain CDs. In the preparation processes, three primary factors (including the dosage of flowers, reaction temperature and reaction time), which affected the fluorescence QY of the CDs, were optimized at first. As shown in Table S1, it can be easily seen that the highest fluorescence QY was determined as 30.8% by using 5 g of the flowers of A.manihot as raw materials, 220 °C as reaction temperature and 4 h as reaction time, respectively. The QY of CDs in this study was higher than the previous reports [2]. The preparation processes of CDs were illustrated in scheme 1.

The optical properties of CDs were explored by fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption. It can be obviously seen from Fig. 1A that CDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence at 410 nm (excited at 330 nm). The CDs solution was almost colorless under daylight and emitted a brighter blue fluorescence under the excitation of 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 1A). Moreover, from Fig. 1B, in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, absorption peaks centered at 280 nm and 310 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum were obtained. As the raw material of CDs are made up by a lot of flavonoids, these two absorption peaks may be due to π-π* electronic transition of CC and n-π* electronic transition of CO existed on CDs surface [49]. Fig. 1C reflected the different emission wavelengths of the CDs which dependent on the different excitation wavelengths (ranged from 310 nm to 390 nm), and the excitation wavelength dependence emission was observed. Furthermore, it can be easily seen from Fig. 1D that as the excitation wavelength increased, the emission wavelength red shifted from 399 nm to 459 nm. This red shift of emission wavelength was attributed to that there were different functional groups on CDs' surface and CDs had different sizes. These factors contributed to variation in fluorescence excitations and satisfied the excitation wavelength dependence [30,35].

The CDs which prepared by using optimum condition were characterized with TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS. The morphology of CDs was obtained by TEM using a dilute aqueous solution of CDs drop-casted on a carbon-coated copper grid. As shown in Fig. 2A, CDs showed spherical morphology and a narrow size distribution from 6 to 11.5 nm with an average size of 9 nm (Fig. 2B). The crystalline nature of the CDs was confirmed by a XRD analysis. The XRD pattern of the CDs in Fig. 1C showed that the broad diffraction peak centred at 2θ = 27.3o was attributed to the (002) lattice spacing of the graphitic carbon, and this indicated that the CDs contained graphite-like structures [50].

Next, FT-IR spectra were applied to identify the functional groups present in CDs. As described in Fig. 2D, the peaks at 3420 and 3170 cm−1 can be attributed to the characteristic absorption bands of the stretching vibration of OH and NH, respectively [51]. Moreover, the peak at 1645 cm−1, 1550 cm−1, 1401 cm−1, 1110 cm−1 can be attributed to CO, CC and the CH2 bending vibration, respectively. The existence of three peaks signals demonstrated that –OH and –COOH may exist on the surface of CDs. Furthermore, the peak at 1100 cm−1 was attributed to CO stretching [52]. In addition, the peak at 2360 cm−1 was identified as the O=C=O stretching vibration. Overall, these peaks of CDs indicated that multiple organic groups, including –COOH, –OH, –C=O, and -NH2 may exist on the surface of CDs.

Further, the surface elements analysis and chemical bonds of CDs were identified by XPS. As shown in Fig. 3A, the surface of CDs was mainly composed of three major elements including C (1 s), N (1 s) and O (1 s). The contents of C1s, N1 s and O1s were identified as 75.59%, 19% and 5.41%, respectively. In Fig. 3B, the high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s can be divide into three peaks. The peak of C1s at 284.5 eV indicated the existence of a CC structure in the CDs, the peaks of C1s at 286.1 eV, 288.0 eV and 292.7 eV can be assigned to C-N/C-O, CO and π-π* transitions, respectively [53]. In Fig. 3C, the spectrum of N1 s was mainly composed of peak of CN (C=N) bonds (399.5 eV), supporting the fact that the nitrogen-containing groups were doped on the CDs [54]. Moreover, in Fig. 3D, the spectrum of O1s can be separated into two peaks at 531.28 eV and 532.38 eV. These two peaks were attributed to CO and C-OH/C-O-C groups, respectively [55]. Above all, multiple organic groups, including the bonds of –COOH, –OH, –C=O, and -NH2 were identified on the surface of CDs. These groups which characterized by XPS were responded to those of FT-IR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the CDs to TNP

ce:section.ce:para: In order to further accurate detect TNP in real samples, it is necessary to optimize the factors (such as pH value, the ionic strength of the solution and solvent for CDs' dispersion) which affect the detection results. As indicated in Fig. 4A and Fig. S2A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increased as pH value increased from 3 to 6. However, when the pH value exceeded 6, the fluorescence intensity became stable and no longer changed. This phenomenon may be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl or amino groups on the surface of the CDs, for which can generate the non-radiative recombination, and consequently enhanced the fluorescence intensity [56,57]. Therefore, pH = 6 was selected as the optimum pH. In order to optimize the ionic strength of the solution, different concentrations (0–1.0 M) of NaCl were added into buffer solution of CDs (pH = 6). As described in Fig. 4B and Fig. S2B, the fluorescence intensity and the peaks of CDs at different concentrations of NaCl had no significant difference, demonstrating that NaCl had almost no influence on the fluorescence stability of CDs. Moreover, it can be clearly seen from Fig. 4C and Fig. S3 (A–E) that the fluorescence intensity of CDs in aqueous solution was higher than those in ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile and acetone, suggesting that water can be selected as the dispersion solvent for CDs. In addition, the anti-photobleaching ability of CDs was also investigated by repeated detection every 1 h for 10 h. As shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence intensity of CDs changes slightly under continuous excitation (330 nm) for 10 h with a UV lamp, which demonstrating that the CDs existed the good property of anti-photobleaching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of CDs to TNP
ce:section.ce:para: To study the sensitivity of CDs in the sensing system, the CDs were applied to TNP sensing. Firstly, the working curve of TNP with CDs was measured and calculated by adding different concentrations (0–100 μM) of TNP into CDs solution. As depicted in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was gradually decreased with the increasing of TNP concentration. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was proportional to the TNP concentration in the range of 25 nM to 40 μM (Fig. 5B). The linear correlation can be calculated as F0/F = 0.0419C + 0.9879 with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.9942. Where F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensities at 330 nm in the presence and absence of TNP, respectively. The C represented the concentration of TNP. As shown in Table S2, the LOD calculated as signal-to-noise ratio > 3 was found to be 5 nM, which is lower than previously reported CDs based sensing methods. Lower LOD highlighted the high sensitivity of CDs which prepared by using the flowers of A.manihot as green precursors.

In addition, the selectivity of CDs for TNP detection was also investigated. The changes of fluorescence intensity of CDs in the existence of various competitive compounds (including TNP, 2-NP, 3-NP, 4-NP, phenol, 2,4-DNA, 2,4-DNP, 2,4-DNT, p-DHB, o-DHB, m-DHB and 1,2,3-THB, 100 μM) were used to evaluate the selectivity of CDs. As shown in Fig. 5C and Fig. S4, the fluorescence response of CDs for TNP was more sensitivity than those of other compounds, demonstrating that the CDs can be used for sensitivity detection of TNP. In addition, the fluorescence responses of CDs for 2,4-DNP, 4-NP, 2,4-DNA and 2-NP were also apparently effective in turn. However, in the presence of the remaining compounds, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs changed only slightly. These results were related to the number of nitro groups on benzene ring in analogues' structure and whether the analogues can form the intramolecular hydrogen bond or not [58]. The more the number of nitro groups on benzene ring in analogues' structure, the stronger the electron deficiency of benzene ring, and this was beneficial to the interaction between CDs (electron donor) and analogues (electron acceptor). TNP contained three nitro groups, which have both inductive and conjugate effects, so the electron-withdrawing effect of TNP was the greatest. Although 2,4-DNA had two nitro groups, the quenching ability to CDs was weaker than that of 4-NP. This was due to the strong electron-rich effect of amino group, which weakened the electron-withdrawing effect of nitro group. Moreover, 4-NP, a typically electron-deficient compound which had both combination of inductive effect and conjugate effect, had a stronger electron-withdrawing effect. In addition, although 4-NP and 2-NP have similar structure, the quenching ability of 4-NP to CDs was obviously greater than that of 2-NP. This phenomenon resulted from that nitro and hydroxyl groups on 2-NP can easily form intramolecular hydrogen bonds, which greatly weaken the electron absorption ability of nitro groups. In conclusion, the fluorescence quenching to CDs was the order of was TNP > DNP > 4-NP > 2,4-DNA > 2-NPs, and the significant fluorescence quenching effect of TNP can make the CDs apply as TNP sensing.

To further evaluate the detection performance of sensitivity and selectivity of CDs in the various real samples, the fluorescence responses of CDs with different metal ions (including Fe3+, Cu2+,Cd2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mg2+ and Fe2+, 100 μM) were also investigated. As indicated in Fig. 5D and Fig. S5. The metal ions had little or no effects on the fluorescence quenching effects of CDs. This result indicated that CDs had selectivity for TNP in the presence of various metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism study of CDs - TNP system
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the interaction between CDs and TNP, some optical properties of CDs were used to explain the reaction mechanism between CDs and TNP. As shown in Fig. 6A, wide spectrum overlapped between fluorescence excitation or emission spectrum of CDs and the absorption spectrum of the quencher (including TNP, 2,4-DNP, 4-NP, 2,4-DNA and 2-NP, except phenol and p-DHB) from 330 nm to 460 nm. These phenomena resulted from that the properties of nitroaromatic compounds such as TNP, 2,4-DNP, 4-NP, 2,4-DNA and 2-NP were quite similar, while phenol and p-DHB had no nitro-group. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 6B, the absorption spectrum of CDs changed obviously in the absence and presence of TNP, 2,4-DNP, 4-NP, 2,4-DNA and 2-NP. This may be caused by the formation of a mixture between this compounds and CDs [22]. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of CDs obviously decreased in presence of these five compounds, and it was obviously seen that the quenching efficiency of TNP is higher than that of the other four nitroaromatic compounds (inset of Fig. 6B). This result may be explained by that the greater electron-deficiency of TNP compared with the other four nitroaromatic compounds [59,60]. However, the other two compounds had no effects on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. All of these results described were consistent with the UV–vis spectra (the overlaps observed between the absorption of quenchers and the excitation or emission spectra of CDs). And the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) may be the quenching mechanism between CDs and nitroaromatic compounds [61].

Fluorescence lifetime measurement was used to further identify the fluorescence quenching mechanism. It can be obviously seen from Fig. 6C and D that the emission decay of CDs was well fitted by a multi-exponential [62] and the fluorescence decay lifetime of CDs (with TNP) was 1.02 ns, which was shorter than that of CDs (3.08 ns). This result indicated that the FRET procedure existed for quenching mechanism [35].

Furthermore, it is reported that the photoluminescence of CDs can be quenched efficiently by ether electron acceptor or electron donor molecules in solution. In other words, the photoexcited CDs can act as both electron donors and electron acceptors. And photoinduced electron transfer (PET) often exist between CDs and molecule (electron donor or acceptor) [63]. In present study, the CDs which prepared by using the flowers of A.manihot as precursor had abundant electron-donating groups on the surface. Therefore, CDs can be acted as electron donor. Moreover, TNP was an electron-deficiency molecule and it can be acted as electron acceptor. Hence, it can be speculated that PET quenching mechanism may exist between CDs and TNP [64]. To confirm the conjecture, the zeta potential of CDs before and after adding TNP was measured. The increment of ξ-value from −10.6 ± 1.2 to −3.6 ± 1.5 mV upon the addition of TNP was observed. The result indicated that the effect of PET occurred in the action of CDs and TNP [65,66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TNP in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the feasibility of the current method for trace analysis of TNP, the detection of TNP by the CDs in tap and lake water was carried out. As no TNP was detected in these water samples, the recovery tests of different concentrations (1, 5 and 10 μM) of TNP in real water samples were applied to evaluate the accuracy and applicability of CDs fluorescent sensor. As shown in Table 1 , satisfactory recoveries ranged from 92.6% to 113% can be obtained by using the TNP sensing platform. The results demonstrated that the CDs can be utilized as a sensitive sensing platform for the detection of TNP in natural water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability assay and cellular imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Cell viability assay is an indispensable parameter for further application of the CDs [11]. To evaluate the cell viability of the CDs, MTT assay was carried out in Hela, A549 and HepG2 cell lines. As depicted in Fig. 7A–C, it can obviously found that the CDs had no obvious cell cytotoxicity on all cells. Even at a high concentration of 500 μg·mL−1, the cell viability was still >95%. These results revealed that the CDs showed low toxicity to the cells, indicating that the CDs have potential value for further biological field applications.

To test the applicability of cellular imaging, Hela cells were incubated with the CDs for 3 h and then the cells were observed by using inverted fluorescence microscopy. The results in Fig. 8A and B showed that before adding CDs, the cells did not emit fluorescence, while after 3 h incubation, the cytoplasm in cells emitted bright blue (Shown in Fig. 8C). The result demonstrated that the as-prepared CDs can easily through the cell membrane and labeled the cytoplasm. A small amount of CDs can even permeated to the nuclei and located at the perinuclear region [67]. However, after further incubation with TNP for 1 h, the fluorescence of cells significantly decreased (Shown in Fig. 8D). These results indicated that the as-prepared CDs can be applied as an effective fluorescence sensor for detecting TNP in bio-system.

In this study, the CDs with high fluorescence QY of 30.8% were successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method with the flowers of A.manihot as raw materials and ultrapure water as solvent for the first time. TEM image clearly showed that CDs had a spherical morphology and a narrow size distribution from 6 to 11.5 nm with an average size of around 9 nm. And the result of XRD further verified the successful preparation of CDs. Multiple organic functional groups on the surface of CDs were further confirmed by using FT-IR and XPS. In addition to the facile synthetic process and high QY, CDs also possessed commendable stability to pH, high ionic strength and photo irradiation. Moreover, CDs exhibited selective linear fluorescence response to TNP in the range of 25 nM- 40 μM with a LOD of 5 nM. FRET and PET were identified as the quenching mechanisms between CDs and TNP. Furthermore, CDs were successfully utilized as a sensitive fluorescent sensor for the detection of TNP in natural water samples and the results were satisfactory. Additionally, due to the low cytotoxicity of CDs, they were successfully used as a nice cellular imaging agent in Hela cells. This result demonstrated that the as-prepared CDs were potential cellular imaging agents.
